title: The temptation of Jesus part 4
scripture_text: ‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: “‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.’ Jesus answered him, ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ ’Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” ‘Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.
devotional:
Jesus had to experience the discipline of a son so he could fulfil His fathers’ purposes for his life.

Often when it comes to the whole area of discipline we have muddled thinking. We are inclined to see discipline as a kind of punishment for sin. However in the mind of God, discipline is not a divine reaction to sin, but an aid to growth and maturity. As a son Jesus never sinned, but as a son he required discipline. The word discipline simply means to educate, or to teach. The Greek word for discipline paideia was used to describe "the process of educating man into his true form, the real and genuine human nature." We often learn more about ourselves and life in time of testing and trial.

The scriptures say,

‘’Remember how the LORD your God led you all the way in the desert these forty years; to humble you and to test you in order to know what was in your heart, whether or not you would keep his commands. He humbled you, causing you to hunger and then feeding you with manna, which neither you nor your fathers had known, to teach you that man does not live on bread alone but on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD. Your clothes did not wear out and your feet did not swell during these forty years. Know then in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD your God disciplines you.’’

Deuteronomy 8:2-5.

This passage must have been on the Lords mind, because twice he quotes from it as he refutes the temptation of Satan.

Were the People of Israel failed in the wilderness, Jesus succeeded.

Jesus had a divinely appointed encounter in the wilderness. Jesus was learning that cost and suffering were part of the life he was called to. His wilderness, hunger, loneliness and temptation were consistent with the love and will of his Father.

“When the world did not end as Jesus himself had said it would, his followers stopped expecting so much from God or from themselves. They hung a wooden cross on the wall and settled back into their more or less comfortable routines, remembering their once passionate devotion to God the way they remembered the other enthusiasms of their youth. Little by little, Christians became devoted to their comforts instead: the soft couch, the flannel sheets, the leg of lamb roasted with rosemary. These things made them feel safe and cared for, if not by God, then by themselves. They decided there was no contradiction between being comfortable and being Christian, and before long it was very hard to pick them out from the population at large. They no longer distinguished themselves by their bold love for one another. They did not get arrested for championing the poor. They blended in. They avoided extremes. They decided to be nice instead of holy, and God moaned out loud.” Barbara Brown Taylor. https://www.religion-online.org/article/settling-for-less-lk-41-13/. Accessed 03/05/25

Are you willing to go to the desert and stay as long as it takes for the Spirit to shape your heart, understanding and mission? Is the desert the part of your Christian life you are trying to avoid or ignore?

The desert is a place where we become vulnerable and thus reliant on God. As Children of God we will all have times and experiences, were we will feel totally vulnerable and exposed. In these times it is easy to feel abandoned by God, you may feel that your faith is fragile, but be assured God is with you and he will use the difficult times to win your heart.
prayer: Father thank you that the reason that you discipline me is because I am your child.
html:
The temptation of Jesus part 4

Study Text: Matthew 4: 1-11
‘’Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry The tempter came to him and said, “If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.” Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” Then the devil took him to the holy city and had him stand on the highest point of the temple. “If you are the Son of God, he said, “throw yourself down. For it is written: “‘He will command his angels concerning you, and they will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.’ Jesus answered him, ‘’It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’ ’Again, the devil took him to a very high mountain and showed him all the kingdoms of the world and their splendour “All this I will give you,” he said, “if you will bow down and worship me.” ‘Jesus said to him, “Away from me, Satan! For it is written: ‘Worship the Lord your God, and serve him only.’ Then the devil left him, and angels came and attended him.

Jesus had to experience the discipline of a son so he could fulfil His fathers’ purposes for his life.

Often when it comes to the whole area of discipline we have muddled thinking. We are inclined to see discipline as a kind of punishment for sin. However in the mind of God, discipline is not a divine reaction to sin, but an aid to growth and maturity. As a son Jesus never sinned, but as a son he required discipline. The word discipline simply means to educate, or to teach. The Greek word for discipline paideia was used to describe "the process of educating man into his true form, the real and genuine human nature." We often learn more about ourselves and life in time of testing and trial.

The scriptures say,

‘’Remember how the LORD your God led you all the way in the desert these forty years; to humble you and to test you in order to know what was in your heart, whether or not you would keep his commands. He humbled you, causing you to hunger and then feeding you with manna, which neither you nor your fathers had known, to teach you that man does not live on bread alone but on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD. Your clothes did not wear out and your feet did not swell during these forty years. Know then in your heart that as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD your God disciplines you.’’

Deuteronomy 8:2-5.

This passage must have been on the Lords mind, because twice he quotes from it as he refutes the temptation of Satan.

Were the People of Israel failed in the wilderness, Jesus succeeded.

Jesus had a divinely appointed encounter in the wilderness. Jesus was learning that cost and suffering were part of the life he was called to. His wilderness, hunger, loneliness and temptation were consistent with the love and will of his Father.

“When the world did not end as Jesus himself had said it would, his followers stopped expecting so much from God or from themselves. They hung a wooden cross on the wall and settled back into their more or less comfortable routines, remembering their once passionate devotion to God the way they remembered the other enthusiasms of their youth. Little by little, Christians became devoted to their comforts instead: the soft couch, the flannel sheets, the leg of lamb roasted with rosemary. These things made them feel safe and cared for, if not by God, then by themselves. They decided there was no contradiction between being comfortable and being Christian, and before long it was very hard to pick them out from the population at large. They no longer distinguished themselves by their bold love for one another. They did not get arrested for championing the poor. They blended in. They avoided extremes. They decided to be nice instead of holy, and God moaned out loud.” Barbara Brown Taylor. https://www.religion-online.org/article/settling-for-less-lk-41-13/. Accessed 03/05/25

Are you willing to go to the desert and stay as long as it takes for the Spirit to shape your heart, understanding and mission? Is the desert the part of your Christian life you are trying to avoid or ignore?

The desert is a place where we become vulnerable and thus reliant on God. As Children of God we will all have times and experiences, were we will feel totally vulnerable and exposed. In these times it is easy to feel abandoned by God, you may feel that your faith is fragile, but be assured God is with you and he will use the difficult times to win your heart.

Prayer: Father thank you that the reason that you discipline me is because I am your child.
Bible in a year reading plan: 2 Samuel 17-19.
